gist_memory.paper_problem_memory: Sporadic vascular malformations (VMs) have high morbidity and limited treatment options. A substantial proportion of patients have no mutations in known associated genes. The study aimed to identify novel genetic causes using ultradeep next-generation sequencing of affected tissue, hypothesizing that mosaic RAS/MAPK pathway variants drive these lesions.

section_gists.heading: Introduction
section_gists.gist: Sporadic VMs are congenital blood vessel malformations with limited treatments. Next-generation sequencing has revealed postzygotic variants in growth pathways, but many patients lack known mutations. The study uses ultradeep sequencing to detect low-frequency mosaic variants in RAS/MAPK genes as a cause.
process_signals: Knowledge gap: substantial proportion of patients have no mutations in known genes
Hypothesis: mosaic RAS/MAPK variants cause sporadic VMs
Approach: ultradeep sequencing to detect low allele frequency variants
methods: Bioinformatic analyses for low mutant allele frequency detection
failures_or_limits: Previous studies failed to find mutations in many patients

section_gists.heading: Results
section_gists.gist: Deep sequencing of 25 high-flow and 135 low-flow VM patients (previously negative for known VM genes) identified mosaic RAS/MAPK variants in 9/25 AVMs (KRAS, BRAF, MAP2K1) and 5/135 low-flow VMs (KRAS, NRAS). Variant allele frequencies were 3-26% in tissue, absent in blood. MAP2K1 variants clustered in exon 2, including a novel deletion. Functional studies showed MAPK pathway activation, increased tube formation, and VM-like phenotypes in zebrafish. Vemurafenib rescued the phenotype.
process_signals: Discovery: mosaic variants in KRAS, BRAF, MAP2K1, NRAS
Validation: variants confirmed by second method, absent in blood
Functional evidence: increased ERK phosphorylation, tube formation
In vivo modeling: zebrafish with BRAF V600E or MAP2K1 Q58del developed VM-like lesions
Therapeutic response: vemurafenib improved blood flow in zebrafish
methods: Deep next-generation sequencing of affected tissue
metrics: Zebrafish phenotype quantification: 15% VM phenotype in BRAF V600E vs 0% in WT
Vemurafenib response: significant improvement in blood flow
failures_or_limits: Low variant allele frequencies (as low as 2-3%)
Some variants not tested in vivo (e.g., MAP2K1 K57N)
Small sample size for drug treatment (n=19)

section_gists.heading: Discussion
section_gists.gist: Mosaic RAS/MAPK variants are a major cause of sporadic VMs, especially high-risk AVMs. Pathogenicity is supported by known cancer variants, mosaic occurrence, and functional assays. The findings align with the 'lethal genes survive by mosaicism' hypothesis. The study opens avenues for targeted therapy repurposing. Limitations include low allele frequencies, FFPE tissue challenges, and need for further in vivo validation.
process_signals: Interpretation: variants are strong activators of MAPK pathway
Context: fits pattern of postzygotic mutations in VM genes
Clinical implication: potential for personalized medicine with MEK/BRAF inhibitors
Limitations acknowledged: low VAF, FFPE artifacts, limited in vivo testing
failures_or_limits: Low variant allele frequencies may lead to false negatives
FFPE tissue may have sequencing artifacts
Not all variants tested in animal models
Drug response only shown in zebrafish, not patients

section_gists.heading: Methods
section_gists.gist: Methods section header only; no process details.

section_gists.heading: Patient cohorts
section_gists.gist: Recruited 23 AVM patients (later 2 intracranial-only added) and 135 low-flow VM patients negative for known VM genes (PIK3CA, AKT1, GNAQ, GNA11, TEK). One additional low-flow patient from a Proteus syndrome trial. Total: 25 high-flow, 135 low-flow.
process_signals: Constraint: initially no known genes for sporadic AVM
Recruitment expanded after initial results (intracranial AVMs)
Low-flow cohort pre-screened negative for known genes
methods: Genotyping for known VM genes before inclusion
failures_or_limits: Possible selection bias from referral center

section_gists.heading: Next-generation sequencing and variant calling
section_gists.gist: Used SureSeq Solid Tumour Panel on FFPE and fresh samples due to biopsy difficulty. Achieved mean 500x depth. For 2 patients, fresh biopsies and paired blood were sequenced. Low-flow patients: targeted capture of 10 genes at 1000x depth. Variants called with custom pipeline, filtered for quality and population frequency, and validated by Sanger or ddPCR.
process_signals: Technical constraint: FFPE tissue required optimized panel
Deep sequencing to detect low-level mosaicism
Validation step: all variants confirmed by orthogonal method
methods: Custom targeted capture for low-flow cohort
Sanger sequencing and ddPCR for validation
failures_or_limits: Panel limited to known cancer genes

section_gists.heading: Mutant plasmid construction and HEK293T and HUVEC transfection
section_gists.gist: Generated MAP2K1 mutants (K57N, Q58del) by site-directed mutagenesis and BRAF V600E by Gateway cloning. Transfected HEK293T and HUVECs for functional assays. Used Lipofectamine 2000 for HEK293T and Lipofectamine LTX with PLUS Reagent for HUVECs.
process_signals: Functional validation of discovered variants
Used standard mutagenesis and transfection protocols
failures_or_limits: Transient transfection may not reflect endogenous expression levels

section_gists.heading: Endothelial cell tube formation assay and microscopy
section_gists.gist: HUVECs transfected with mutant or WT constructs were seeded on Geltrex. Tube formation was assessed at 14h by calcein AM staining and ImageJ angiogenesis analyzer. Mutants showed increased tube formation.
process_signals: Functional readout for angiogenesis
Quantitative analysis using ImageJ
failures_or_limits: In vitro assay may not fully recapitulate in vivo VM

section_gists.heading: Transgenic zebrafish
section_gists.gist: Generated fli1a promoter-driven constructs for BRAF WT/V600E and MAP2K1 WT/Q58del. Injected into 1-cell stage fli1a:GFP embryos with Tol2 mRNA to create mosaic expression. Screened for VM phenotypes.
process_signals: In vivo model to test variant pathogenicity
Mosaic expression mimics human mosaicism
failures_or_limits: Only tested BRAF V600E and MAP2K1 Q58del

section_gists.heading: Drug treatments
section_gists.gist: Zebrafish embryos with VM phenotype were treated with 0.1 μM vemurafenib from 2.5 dpf, refreshed daily. Controls received DMSO. Blood flow improvement was scored blindly. Vemurafenib significantly rescued the phenotype.
process_signals: Proof-of-concept for targeted therapy
Blinded scoring to reduce bias
methods: Live imaging with Leica stereo and confocal microscopy
failures_or_limits: Only tested BRAF inhibitor, not MEK inhibitor

global_hypotheses_about_storyline: Initial problem: many sporadic VMs have unknown genetic cause, limiting treatment options.
Hypothesis: mosaic activating variants in RAS/MAPK pathway genes drive these malformations.
Approach: use ultradeep sequencing of affected tissue to detect low-frequency variants.
Discovery: identified mosaic variants in KRAS, BRAF, MAP2K1, NRAS in both high-flow and low-flow VMs.
Validation: functional assays confirmed MAPK pathway activation and increased angiogenesis.
Translation: zebrafish model recapitulated VM phenotype and responded to BRAF inhibitor, suggesting therapeutic potential.
Limitations: low VAF, FFPE artifacts, limited in vivo testing, small drug treatment cohorts.
uncertainties_to_resolve: Were any variants initially missed due to low coverage or FFPE artifacts?
Did the researchers test other RAS/MAPK genes before focusing on these four?
Were there any negative results in functional assays not reported?
How were the specific variants chosen for in vivo modeling?
Was there any attempt to treat patients with targeted therapy based on these findings?
What is the long-term outcome of vemurafenib treatment in the zebrafish model?

evidence_bank.quote: Sporadic vascular malformations (VMs) are complex congenital anomalies of blood vessels that lead to stroke, life-threatening bleeds, disfigurement, overgrowth, and/or pain.
Therapeutic options are severely limited, and multidisciplinary management remains challenging, particularly for high-flow arteriovenous malformations (AVM).
a substantial proportion of patients have no mutations in known associated genes
We sought to address this issue using ultradeep next-generation sequencing and bioinformatic analyses aimed at detection of low mutant allele frequency in affected tissue.
Here, we discover that multiple mosaic-activating variants in 4 genes of the RAS/MAPK pathway cause VM and model therapy in an animal model.

To investigate the pathogenesis of sporadic intracranial and extracranial VMs in 160 children in which known genetic causes had been excluded, we sequenced DNA from affected tissue and optimized analysis for detection of low mutant allele frequency.
Using deep next-generation sequencing of affected tissue, known or predicted pathogenic variants in RAS/MAPK pathway genes were identified in 9 of the 25 patients with AVMs
Identified variants were in KRAS (n = 4) and BRAF (n = 1)
a cluster of variants was identified in exon 2 of MAP2K1 (n = 4)
confirmed by a second method
In vitro characterization and 2 transgenic zebrafish AVM models that recapitulated the human phenotype validated the pathogenesis of the mutant alleles.
treatment of AVM-BRAF mutant zebrafish with the BRAF inhibitor vemurafinib restored blood flow in AVM
These variants were more frequent in high-flow than low-flow VMs.
Using deep next-generation sequencing of affected tissue, known or predicted pathogenic variants in RAS/MAPK pathway genes were identified in 9 of the 25 patients with AVMs
Of the 135 patients with low-flow VMs, 5 had mosaic RAS gene variants, 4 in KRAS and 1 in NRAS
Variant allele frequencies ranged from 3% to 26% in affected tissue
All were found to affect helix A in the 3D protein structure (residues 44–58).
these variants are therefore predicted to destabilize the conformation of the inactive state.

Transient overexpression of MAP2K1 and BRAF mutants in HEK293T cells led to increased phosphorylation of ERK compared to either overexpression of corresponding WT genes or to mock-transfected controls at 24 hours
Overexpression of 2 of the same mutants in HUVEC led to disruption and disordering of spontaneous vascular tube formation between 6 and 24 hours compared with mock-transfected controls
significant reductions in mean number of master junctions, total vascular tube length, and total mesh area
almost all vemurafenib-treated zebrafish exhibited improved blood flow
confirmed by a second method
We hypothesize that congenital VMs ultimately result from dysregulation of vascular cell MAPK and/or PI3K signaling during human embryonic development.

In silico protein-interaction analysis (34) of the full spectrum of genes associated with VMs, including our new findings, demonstrates a strong network of functional interaction among the gene products (Supplemental Figure 2).
Analysis of multiple different affected tissues in 2 of the patients revealed consistency of mutation within an individual, confirming a postzygotic hit to a multipotent precursor in those cases.

The cause of the difference in frequency of these variants between the high- and low-flow cohorts is not clear. As a minority of the low-flow cohort skin biopsies were cultured for fibroblasts before DNA extraction, it is possible that the mutations were either lost in culture or not present in fibroblasts.
mosaic mutations in a third of our AVM cohort, including intracranial AVMs, will radically alter the concept of management of these VMs by introducing the possibility of targeted medical therapy.
Somatic variants in MAP2K1 cluster in exon 2 and are predicted to destabilize the 3D structure of the inactive form of the kinase.
Targeted next-generation sequencing was performed on affected tissue DNA using a custom panel of overgrowth-related genes on an Illumina MiSeq platform.
This panel was designed for the low-flow study to create a selection of possible candidate genes for overgrowth on the basis of the genes/pathways already known to be involved in this phenotype.
VCF files tailored for mosaic variant calling were created in MiSeq Reporter (Illumina) and annotated in Illumina Variant Studio, version 2.2, resulting in a list of 300–1,000 variants per sample.

initial_request.research_problem: Sporadic vascular malformations (VMs) have high morbidity and limited treatment options, and a substantial proportion of patients have no mutations in known associated genes.
initial_request.known_context: Therapeutic options are severely limited, and multidisciplinary management remains challenging, particularly for high-flow arteriovenous malformations (AVM).

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Sporadic VMs have high morbidity and limited treatment options, and many patients lack mutations in known genes.

evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Design study to test hypothesis using ultradeep sequencing.

evidence.quote_or_span: Sporadic vascular malformations (VMs) are complex congenital anomalies of blood vessels that lead to stroke, life-threatening bleeds, disfigurement, overgrowth, and/or pain.
Therapeutic options are severely limited, and multidisciplinary management remains challenging, particularly for high-flow arteriovenous malformations (AVM).
a substantial proportion of patients have no mutations in known associated genes
Here, we discover that multiple mosaic-activating variants in 4 genes of the RAS/MAPK pathway cause VM and model therapy in an animal model.

success_verification.final_conclusion: Mosaic RAS/MAPK variants cause sporadic VMs, and targeted therapy with vemurafenib rescues the phenotype in a zebrafish model, suggesting potential for medical therapy in patients.
limitations: Unexplained difference in variant frequency between high- and low-flow cohorts; possible loss in fibroblast culture.
